input.description: Extract notifications subtree where Reckoner lives

content.content:
# c-mo1z3teo-6d48c8e33aa9 [live]
> Implement notifications when the system requires human intervention
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-16T21:10:16.992Z

## c-mob1vyb2-14c54db7c872 [concluded]
> What events should trigger an intervention notification?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: MVP triggers: any A-level blocked transition (commission reaches terminal non-success: stuck / failed / retry-cap-exhausted), plus D13 queue-drained. Additional A-items (budget exhausted, dispatch-failed, seal-conflict, system-unhealthy) light up as their underlying mechanisms ship. Skip B (delivered-side) for now — B7 needs-review flag has no mechanism to hook. Frame: notifications fire on transitions into Blocked or Delivered; MVP covers Blocked + drain only.
Created: 2026-04-23T05:38:04.526Z
Resolved: 2026-04-23T05:54:15.743Z

## c-mob1vyyi-52f6a95639a4 [concluded]
> What channel(s) should intervention notifications be delivered on?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: MVP channels: Discord webhook (push, primary) + CLI inbox (nsg pulse list, durable query and fallback). The Lattice implements a channel abstraction so future channels (ntfy, Pushover, email, Matrix, desktop) slot in without refactoring. Fan-out: every pulse to every registered channel; routing deferred to later. Rejected: ntfy (fine, but Discord is zero net-new infra since patron already uses it), Pushover (paid + another app, no meaningful reliability edge at our volume), email (too async for blocked-commission signals), desktop OS notification (devcontainer→host bridge plumbing, doesn't help when patron is away from the machine). Channel config (webhook URLs, secrets, config schema) deferred to the planning rig.
Created: 2026-04-23T05:38:05.370Z
Resolved: 2026-04-23T19:27:40.805Z

## c-mob1vzjy-8930470f5154 [concluded]
> What should a notification payload contain?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Pulse fields: id, source (string — emitter: reckoner, coinmaster, anima, ...), trigger_type (namespaced string — writ.stuck, queue.drained, ...), writ_id (nullable — the subject), title (one-line headline), summary (few lines of human context), link_url (nullable), context (nullable JSON blob), created_at, delivery_state (pending | delivered | failed), ack_state (pending | acked | dismissed | resolved), acked_at (nullable). Strings over enums for trigger_type to keep the Lattice extensible to future producers; channels do best-effort rendering with fallback on unknown types. Rejected for MVP: severity (derivable from trigger_type), recipient/target (patron-only in MVP), correlation_id (no intermediate signals since we trigger on terminal states only), expires_at / grouped_from / parent_pulse_id (rate-limit click territory), per-channel delivery tracking (one real push channel — revisit when multi-channel routing matters). Schema details (column types, indexes, JSON storage strategy) deferred to the planning rig.
Created: 2026-04-23T05:38:06.142Z
Resolved: 2026-04-23T20:46:36.626Z

## c-mob1w04q-7df77f7562d6 [live]
> What response surface should a notification offer the patron?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-23T05:38:06.890Z

## c-mob1w0ql-10ebbb35a027 [live]
> How should notifications handle rate limiting and digesting?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-23T05:38:07.677Z

## c-mob1w13b-91635892d2fe [concluded]
> What is the authoritative signal source for 'needs intervention'?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Source: writ-state CDC. The Reckoner (guild command-and-control apparatus; see also c-moa42rxh vision-keeper and c-moaj06ty overseer) observes writ-state transitions and emits a pulse — a durable record carrying delivery + ack state — into the Lattice (general-purpose guild messaging apparatus), which delivers directly to the patron. Queue-drained derived in-stream on each terminal transition (check remaining runnable work, emit drain pulse if empty). Writ model stays pure — ack lives on the pulse, not the writ. Rejected: writ-level ack flags, Clockworks events as source (ephemeral), rig-level events (blocks on c-mo3ibj94), inline emit (scatters logic), polling (loses precision).
Created: 2026-04-23T05:38:08.136Z
Resolved: 2026-04-23T18:20:08.552Z

content.text:
Here's what I found, Sean — the Reckoner doesn't have its own dedicated click thread yet. It emerged as a design decision **inside** the notifications subtree (`c-mo1z3teo` — "Implement notifications when the system requires human intervention"), and it shows up in two concluded clicks:

## Direct Reckoner references

**`c-mob1w13b`** *(concluded)* — "What is the authoritative signal source for 'needs intervention'?"
- Concluded that the **Reckoner** is the guild command-and-control apparatus that observes writ-state transitions via CDC and emits pulses into the Lattice.
- Cross-references `c-moa42rxh` (vision-keeper) and `c-moaj06ty` (overseer) as related apparatuses.

**`c-mob1vzjy`** *(concluded)* — "What should a notification payload contain?"
- Defined the pulse schema. Lists `reckoner` as one of the `source` emitter values (alongside `coinmaster`, `anima`, etc.) — implying the Reckoner is expected to be a stable pulse producer.

## Adjacent / related threads still live

Under the same notifications subtree, two sibling clicks remain open and are likely to need Reckoner involvement:
- **`c-mob1w04q`** — response surface for pulses (what can the patron *do* with a notification).
- **`c-mob1w0ql`** — rate limiting and digesting of pulses.

And the referenced cousins:
- **`c-moa42rxh`** — design the vision-keeper role.
- **`c-moaj06ty`** — design the overseer pattern.

## Observation

The Reckoner has a *name*, a *role* (command-and-control apparatus observing writ-state), and a *first job* (emit intervention pulses), but no click dedicated to designing it as a standing apparatus. If we're going to build it out beyond "thing that emits writ.stuck pulses," we probably want a proper design click — something like *"Design the Reckoner apparatus — scope, responsibilities, relationship to vision-keeper/overseer."*

Want me to open one?

content.text:
Here's the Stage 3 subtree (`c-moa42fn6` — *Self-commissioning*), Sean:

## Live children

**Direct children (4):**

1. **`c-mo1mqgqv`** — Design first-class internal commission flow (Petition)
2. **`c-mo3hinjt`** — Design how the system surfaces and performs its own improvements — noticing work that needs doing and commissioning it without patron prompting
   - **`c-mo39ro9x`** *(grandchild)* — Turn analyst observations into follow-up commissions — should observations be first-class sources of new commissionable work, and how?
3. **`c-moa42rxh`** — Design the vision-keeper role — holds a forward-looking product vision and decomposes it into commissionable work
4. **`c-moaj06ty`** — Design the overseer pattern: standing process that observes system state and creates work without patron prompting
   - **`c-moaj0a7u`** *(grandchild)* — Rule-based vs. LLM-observer vs. hybrid: what is the right evaluation shape for the overseer?
   - **`c-moaz1pdw`** *(grandchild)* — Ship Astrolabe changes: structured PlanDoc observations + post-planning step that creates draft writs per observation
   - **`c-moaz1q9b`** *(grandchild)* — Ship MVP Overseer: `nsg overseer commission` — promote one draft writ from the new pool to open
   - **`c-moaz1r58`** *(grandchild)* — Measure observation-draft duplication post-hoc via cancelled-writ analysis and small-diff sealed-commit analysis once MVP has produced data

## Dropped / superseded

Three clicks under the overseer branch were dropped when scope sharpened on 2026-04-23 — the old framing used clicks-substrate / Ratchet-gated lift-to-clicks; the new framing uses structured `PlanDoc.observations` becoming draft mandate writs directly. The replacements are the three `c-moaz1...` clicks above.

## Shape of the stage

Four independent design threads, with meaningful convergence between 3 and 4:
- **Petition** (how internal commissions flow through the books)
- **Self-improvement noticing** (systemic observation → commission)
- **Vision-keeper** (long-horizon product vision → decomposition)
- **Overseer** (short-horizon system-state observation → promotion)

The Overseer thread is the one with the most concrete "ship it" clicks underneath — the rest are still at the design-question stage.

message.content: I would like to say that "Reckoner" is the new term for "Overseer". That is the apparatus through which the system generates its own commissions -- either related to tech debt, issue remediation, or accomplishing the vision. I would say that the notification piece, in particular, may or may not be the responsibility of 'The Reckoner' even though it sounds like the clicsk have conflated taht. With that in mind, where are we at the work to turn observations into commissions/

1	# Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission
2	
3	## Intent
4	
5	Promote Astrolabe's `PlanDoc.observations` from free-form prose to a structured array, and teach the planning rig to emit one draft writ per observation. Each generated writ is a regular, commissionable writ in `new` status, parented under the brief the observations came from, and ready to be promoted to `open` by downstream curators (patron or overseer — see `c-moaz1q9b`).
6	
7	This turns the sage's "things we noticed but didn't action" output from an inert note buried in a plandoc into a proper commissionable draft, visible in the same writ surfaces as any other draft brief.
8	
9	## Motivation
10	
11	Today the `observations` field on a `PlanDoc` is a `string` blob of prose. The sage records real, specific signal there — refactoring opportunities, doc drift, latent bugs, follow-up commissions — but nothing downstream consumes it. A scan of recent plandocs showed observations are already written at mini-brief quality: they name specific files and symbols, identify the work, and note preconditions. What's missing is a mechanism to turn each observation into a commissionable draft without the patron re-authoring them by hand.
12	
13	Making observations into `new`-status writs gives them a standard lifecycle: patron/overseer promotes to `open`, cancels with reason, or leaves in `new` indefinitely. No new substrate is required — the writ lifecycle already expresses all of this.
14	
15	Design decisions informing this work trace to `c-moaz1pdw` (this commission's design click) and `c-moaj06ty` (overseer pattern parent).
16	
17	## Non-negotiable decisions
18	
19	### PlanDoc observations become a structured array
20	
21	`PlanDoc.observations` changes from a prose string to an array of observation records. Each record carries at minimum a plandoc-local id, a title, and a body.
22	
23	Shape sketch:
24	
25	    observations: [
26	      { id: "obs-1", title: "...", body: "..." },
27	      { id: "obs-2", title: "...", body: "..." }
28	    ]
29	
30	Ids are stable within a plandoc. Cross-plandoc uniqueness is not required.
31	
32	No status field. No addressedBy. The plandoc is the authoring artifact; the generated writ carries lifecycle.
33	
34	### Sage-writer update: title per observation, body stays as-is
35	
36	The sage role responsible for emitting `PlanDoc.observations` gets its role instructions updated to emit the structured shape. Each observation becomes one record with a short title and a body.
37	
38	Body content does not require a register change. Today's observations already name specific files, line numbers, symbols, and preconditions — that tactical level of detail is preserved verbatim in the body. The instruction change is: split what's currently one prose blob into one-record-per-concern, and give each a one-line title.
39	
40	What counts as "one observation" is the sage's judgment — the atomicity guidance stays the same as current practice (one concern per bullet). The structural change is packaging, not substance.
41	
42	### Post-planning step creates one writ per observation
43	
44	After the spec-writer completes and the plandoc reaches its final state, a new post-planning step walks `plandoc.observations` and creates one writ per observation.
45	
46	- **Writ type**: `brief`. Matches the current combined planning+implementation rig mapping. Parallel click `c-moaz1sot` may later collapse `brief` into `mandate`; when that lands, this commission's generated writs get migrated alongside everything else as part of that work — not this one.
47	- **Writ status**: `new`. Not dispatched; awaiting promotion.
48	- **Writ title**: `observation.title`.
49	- **Writ body**: `observation.body` verbatim.
50	- **Parent**: the brief writ the plandoc was planning. The generated writ is a child of that writ in the Clerk's tree.
51	
52	No special link types, no new writ types, no cross-substrate bookkeeping. The generated writ is a regular draft writ that happens to have been authored by a sage rather than a patron.
53	
54	### No dependency on any other plugin
55	
56	This work touches only Astrolabe. Clerk is already a hard dependency. No Ratchet interaction, no new link types, no new kit contributions.
57	
58	## Out of scope
59	
60	- **Observation dedup of any kind.** No dedup key generation, no sibling/tree scan, no consultation of cancelled writs for prior-decline signal. Duplicates will accumulate; they are acceptable at MVP and will be measured post-hoc from existing data. Do not invent a dedup layer in this commission.
61	- **Brief-register rewriting of observation bodies.** The sage does not reshape observation content into a formal brief structure. Today's body style is preserved. The only content change is "one record per concern" and the title.
62	- **Observation lifecycle tracking beyond standard writ lifecycle.** Whether a generated writ is promoted, cancelled, or ignored is visible through its own status. No separate observation-status field anywhere.
63	- **The overseer consumer.** Designed and shipped separately under `c-moaz1q9b`. This commission delivers the *input* the overseer will consume; it does not deliver the overseer. Patron can also promote drafts by hand; overseer is a convenience, not a prerequisite.
64	- **Schema evolution for existing plandocs.** If any existing plandocs carry the old string shape, they're left alone — the structured shape applies to new plandocs only. No migration pass.
65	- **Post-planning step as a general extension point.** This is a specific step for observation-lifting. Whether Astrolabe should have a general post-planning plugin-hook surface is a separate design concern and not in scope.
66	- **The `brief`/`mandate` consolidation question.** Tracked under `c-moaz1sot` and handled there. This commission hardcodes `brief` for the generated writ type.
67	
68	## Behavioral cases the design depends on
69	
70	- A plandoc with three observations produces three writs after the post-planning step, each in `new` status, each parented under the original brief writ, each carrying its observation's title and body.
71	- A plandoc with zero observations (empty array or missing field) produces no writs and no errors.
72	- The generated writ's type is `brief`. Post-`c-moaz1sot`, this value updates in lockstep with the rest of the codebase.
73	- A plandoc with the old string-shaped `observations` field is not processed by the new step (no lift, no error). The step only recognizes the structured array shape.
74	- Promoting one of the generated writs through the normal `new → open` transition triggers Spider dispatch via the same machinery that handles any other draft writ.
75	- Cancelling one of the generated writs terminates it with no side effects on the source plandoc — the observation record in the plandoc remains unchanged.
76	
77	## References
78	
79	- `c-moaz1pdw` — this commission's design click
80	- `c-moaj06ty` — overseer pattern design parent
81	- `c-moaz1q9b` — sibling commission: MVP Overseer (consumer)
82	- `c-moaz1r58` — sibling click: post-hoc duplication measurement methodology
83	- `c-moaz1sot` — adjacent click: `brief` writ type removal. This commission hardcodes `brief`; the removal commission updates it in lockstep.
84	- `c-moa42fn6` — Stage 3 umbrella: Self-commissioning

file.content:
# Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission

## Intent

Promote Astrolabe's `PlanDoc.observations` from free-form prose to a structured array, and teach the planning rig to emit one draft writ per observation. Each generated writ is a regular, commissionable writ in `new` status, parented under the brief the observations came from, and ready to be promoted to `open` by downstream curators (patron or overseer — see `c-moaz1q9b`).

This turns the sage's "things we noticed but didn't action" output from an inert note buried in a plandoc into a proper commissionable draft, visible in the same writ surfaces as any other draft brief.

## Motivation

Today the `observations` field on a `PlanDoc` is a `string` blob of prose. The sage records real, specific signal there — refactoring opportunities, doc drift, latent bugs, follow-up commissions — but nothing downstream consumes it. A scan of recent plandocs showed observations are already written at mini-brief quality: they name specific files and symbols, identify the work, and note preconditions. What's missing is a mechanism to turn each observation into a commissionable draft without the patron re-authoring them by hand.

Making observations into `new`-status writs gives them a standard lifecycle: patron/overseer promotes to `open`, cancels with reason, or leaves in `new` indefinitely. No new substrate is required — the writ lifecycle already expresses all of this.

Design decisions informing this work trace to `c-moaz1pdw` (this commission's design click) and `c-moaj06ty` (overseer pattern parent).

## Non-negotiable decisions

### PlanDoc observations become a structured array

`PlanDoc.observations` changes from a prose string to an array of observation records. Each record carries at minimum a plandoc-local id, a title, and a body.

Shape sketch:

    observations: [
      { id: "obs-1", title: "...", body: "..." },
      { id: "obs-2", title: "...", body: "..." }
    ]

Ids are stable within a plandoc. Cross-plandoc uniqueness is not required.

No status field. No addressedBy. The plandoc is the authoring artifact; the generated writ carries lifecycle.

### Sage-writer update: title per observation, body stays as-is

The sage role responsible for emitting `PlanDoc.observations` gets its role instructions updated to emit the structured shape. Each observation becomes one record with a short title and a body.

Body content does not require a register change. Today's observations already name specific files, line numbers, symbols, and preconditions — that tactical level of detail is preserved verbatim in the body. The instruction change is: split what's currently one prose blob into one-record-per-concern, and give each a one-line title.

What counts as "one observation" is the sage's judgment — the atomicity guidance stays the same as current practice (one concern per bullet). The structural change is packaging, not substance.

### Post-planning step creates one writ per observation

After the spec-writer completes and the plandoc reaches its final state, a new post-planning step walks `plandoc.observations` and creates one writ per observation.

- **Writ type**: `brief`. Matches the current combined planning+implementation rig mapping. Parallel click `c-moaz1sot` may later collapse `brief` into `mandate`; when that lands, this commission's generated writs get migrated alongside everything else as part of that work — not this one.
- **Writ status**: `new`. Not dispatched; awaiting promotion.
- **Writ title**: `observation.title`.
- **Writ body**: `observation.body` verbatim.
- **Parent**: the brief writ the plandoc was planning. The generated writ is a child of that writ in the Clerk's tree.

No special link types, no new writ types, no cross-substrate bookkeeping. The generated writ is a regular draft writ that happens to have been authored by a sage rather than a patron.

### No dependency on any other plugin

This work touches only Astrolabe. Clerk is already a hard dependency. No Ratchet interaction, no new link types, no new kit contributions.

## Out of scope

- **Observation dedup of any kind.** No dedup key generation, no sibling/tree scan, no consultation of cancelled writs for prior-decline signal. Duplicates will accumulate; they are acceptable at MVP and will be measured post-hoc from existing data. Do not invent a dedup layer in this commission.
- **Brief-register rewriting of observation bodies.** The sage does not reshape observation content into a formal brief structure. Today's body style is preserved. The only content change is "one record per concern" and the title.
- **Observation lifecycle tracking beyond standard writ lifecycle.** Whether a generated writ is promoted, cancelled, or ignored is visible through its own status. No separate observation-status field anywhere.
- **The overseer consumer.** Designed and shipped separately under `c-moaz1q9b`. This commission delivers the *input* the overseer will consume; it does not deliver the overseer. Patron can also promote drafts by hand; overseer is a convenience, not a prerequisite.
- **Schema evolution for existing plandocs.** If any existing plandocs carry the old string shape, they're left alone — the structured shape applies to new plandocs only. No migration pass.
- **Post-planning step as a general extension point.** This is a specific step for observation-lifting. Whether Astrolabe should have a general post-planning plugin-hook surface is a separate design concern and not in scope.
- **The `brief`/`mandate` consolidation question.** Tracked under `c-moaz1sot` and handled there. This commission hardcodes `brief` for the generated writ type.

## Behavioral cases the design depends on

- A plandoc with three observations produces three writs after the post-planning step, each in `new` status, each parented under the original brief writ, each carrying its observation's title and body.
- A plandoc with zero observations (empty array or missing field) produces no writs and no errors.
- The generated writ's type is `brief`. Post-`c-moaz1sot`, this value updates in lockstep with the rest of the codebase.
- A plandoc with the old string-shaped `observations` field is not processed by the new step (no lift, no error). The step only recognizes the structured array shape.
- Promoting one of the generated writs through the normal `new → open` transition triggers Spider dispatch via the same machinery that handles any other draft writ.
- Cancelling one of the generated writs terminates it with no side effects on the source plandoc — the observation record in the plandoc remains unchanged.

## References

- `c-moaz1pdw` — this commission's design click
- `c-moaj06ty` — overseer pattern design parent
- `c-moaz1q9b` — sibling commission: MVP Overseer (consumer)
- `c-moaz1r58` — sibling click: post-hoc duplication measurement methodology
- `c-moaz1sot` — adjacent click: `brief` writ type removal. This commission hardcodes `brief`; the removal commission updates it in lockstep.
- `c-moa42fn6` — Stage 3 umbrella: Self-commissioning

- The generated writ's type is `brief`. Post-`c-moaz1sot`, this value updates in lockstep with the rest of the codebase.
- A plandoc with the old string-shaped `observations` field is not processed by the new step (no lift, no error). The step only recognizes the structured array shape.
- Promoting one of the generated writs through the normal `new → open` transition triggers Spider dispatch via the same machinery that handles any other draft writ.
- Cancelling one of the generated writs terminates it with no side effects on the source plandoc — the observation record in the plandoc remains unchanged.

## References

- `c-moaz1pdw` — this commission's design click
- `c-moaj06ty` — overseer pattern design parent
- `c-moaz1q9b` — sibling commission: MVP Overseer (consumer)
- `c-moaz1r58` — sibling click: post-hoc duplication measurement methodology
- `c-moaz1sot` — adjacent click: `brief` writ type removal. This commission hardcodes `brief`; the removal commission updates it in lockstep.

toolUseResult.stdout:
{
  "id": "w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2",
  "type": "brief",
  "phase": "completed",
  "title": "Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission",
  "body": "# Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission

## Intent

Promote Astrolabe's `PlanDoc.observations` from free-form prose to a structured array, and teach the planning rig to emit one draft writ per observation. Each generated writ is a regular, commissionable writ in `new` status, parented under the brief the observations came from, and ready to be promoted to `open` by downstream curators (patron or overseer — see `c-moaz1q9b`).

This turns the sage's "things we noticed but didn't action" output from an inert note buried in a plandoc into a proper commissionable draft, visible in the same writ surfaces as any other draft brief.

## Motivation

Today the `observations` field on a `PlanDoc` is a `string` blob of prose. The sage records real, specific signal there — refactoring opportunities, doc drift, latent bugs, follow-up commissions — but nothing downstream consumes it. A scan of recent plandocs showed observations are already written at mini-brief quality: they name specific files and symbols, identify the work, and note preconditions. What's missing is a mechanism to turn each observation into a commissionable draft without the patron re-authoring them by hand.

Making observations into `new`-status writs gives them a standard lifecycle: patron/overseer promotes to `open`, cancels with reason, or leaves in `new` indefinitely. No new substrate is required — the writ lifecycle already expresses all of this.

Design decisions informing this work trace to `c-moaz1pdw` (this commission's design click) and `c-moaj06ty` (overseer pattern parent).

## Non-negotiable decisions

### PlanDoc observations become a structured array

`PlanDoc.observations` changes from a prose string to an array of observation records. Each record carries at minimum a plandoc-local id, a title, and a body.

Shape sketch:

    observations: [
      { id: "obs-1", title: "...", body: "..." },
      { id: "obs-2", title: "...", body: "..." }
    ]

Ids are stable within a plandoc. Cross-plandoc uniqueness is not required.

No status field. No addressedBy. The plandoc is the authoring artifact; the generated writ carries lifecycle.

### Sage-writer update: title per observation, body stays as-is

The sage role responsible for emitting `PlanDoc.observations` gets its role instructions updated to emit the structured shape. Each observation becomes one record with a short title and a body.

Body content does not require a register change. Today's observations already name specific files, line numbers, symbols, and preconditions — that tactical level of detail is preserved verbatim in the body. The instruction change is: split what's currently one prose blob into one-record-per-concern, and give each a one-line title.

What counts as "one observation" is the sage's judgment — the atomicity guidance stays the same as current practice (one concern per bullet). The structural change is packaging, not substance.

### Post-planning step creates one writ per observation

After the spec-writer completes and the plandoc reaches its final state, a new post-planning step walks `plandoc.observations` and creates one writ per observation.

- **Writ type**: `brief`. Matches the current combined planning+implementation rig mapping. Parallel click `c-moaz1sot` may later collapse `brief` into `mandate`; when that lands, this commission's generated writs get migrated alongside everything else as part of that work — not this one.
- **Writ status**: `new`. Not dispatched; awaiting promotion.
- **Writ title**: `observation.title`.
- **Writ body**: `observation.body` verbatim.
- **Parent**: the brief writ the plandoc was planning. The generated writ is a child of that writ in the Clerk's tree.

No special link types, no new writ types, no cross-substrate bookkeeping. The generated writ is a regular draft writ that happens to have been authored by a sage rather than a patron.

### No dependency on any other plugin

This work touches only Astrolabe. Clerk is already a hard dependency. No Ratchet interaction, no new link types, no new kit contributions.

## Out of scope

- **Observation dedup of any kind.** No dedup key generation, no sibling/tree scan, no consultation of cancelled writs for prior-decline signal. Duplicates will accumulate; they are acceptable at MVP and will be measured post-hoc from existing data. Do not invent a dedup layer in this commission.
- **Brief-register rewriting of observation bodies.** The sage does not reshape observation content into a formal brief structure. Today's body style is preserved. The only content change is "one record per concern" and the title.
- **Observation lifecycle tracking beyond standard writ lifecycle.** Whether a generated writ is promoted, cancelled, or ignored is visible through its own status. No separate observation-status field anywhere.
- **The overseer consumer.** Designed and shipped separately under `c-moaz1q9b`. This commission delivers the *input* the overseer will consume; it does not deliver the overseer. Patron can also promote drafts by hand; overseer is a convenience, not a prerequisite.
- **Schema evolution for existing plandocs.** If any existing plandocs carry the old string shape, they're left alone — the structured shape applies to new plandocs only. No migration pass.
- **Post-planning step as a general extension point.** This is a specific step for observation-lifting. Whether Astrolabe should have a general post-planning plugin-hook surface is a separate design concern and not in scope.
- **The `brief`/`mandate` consolidation question.** Tracked under `c-moaz1sot` and handled there. This commission hardcodes `brief` for the generated writ type.

## Behavioral cases the design depends on

- A plandoc with three observations produces three writs after the post-planning step, each in `new` status, each parented under the original brief writ, each carrying its observation's title and body.
- A plandoc with zero observations (empty array or missing field) produces no writs and no errors.
- The generated writ's type is `brief`. Post-`c-moaz1sot`, this value updates in lockstep with the rest of the codebase.
- A plandoc with the old string-shaped `observations` field is not processed by the new step (no lift, no error). The step only recognizes the structured array shape.
- Promoting one of the generated writs through the normal `new → open` transition triggers Spider dispatch via the same machinery that handles any other draft writ.
- Cancelling one of the generated writs terminates it with no side effects on the source plandoc — the observation record in the plandoc remains unchanged.

## References

- `c-moaz1pdw` — this commission's design click
- `c-moaj06ty` — overseer pattern design parent
- `c-moaz1q9b` — sibling commission: MVP Overseer (consumer)
- `c-moaz1r58` — sibling click: post-hoc duplication measurement methodology
- `c-moaz1sot` — adjacent click: `brief` writ type removal. This commission hardcodes `brief`; the removal commission updates it in lockstep.
- `c-moa42fn6` — Stage 3 umbrella: Self-commissioning",

lastPrompt: I would like to say that "Reckoner" is the new term for "Overseer". That is the apparatus through which the system generates its own commissions -- either related to tech debt, issue remediation, or a…

content.text:
Got it — **Reckoner = Overseer**, and the Reckoner is the self-commissioning apparatus (tech debt + issue remediation + vision pursuit). Notifications are a separate concern that got entangled in the clicks. Clean framing.

## State of observations → commissions

**The input half is SHIPPED.** 🎯

- `c-moaz1pdw` — *Ship Astrolabe structured PlanDoc observations + post-planning draft-writ emission*
- Commissioned as writ `w-moaz6k39` ("Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission")
- **Completed and sealed** — commit `ad65df3c` on 2026-04-23
- The sage now emits structured `observations: [{id, title, body}]`, and a post-planning step creates one `brief`-type writ in `new` status per observation, parented under the originating brief

So the pipe exists: planning sessions now leave behind draft commissions as a side-effect.

**The output half is UNSTARTED.**

- `c-moaz1q9b` — *Ship MVP Reckoner: `nsg overseer commission` — promote one draft writ from `new` to `open`*
- No brief drafted, no writ posted
- This is the obvious next move: something needs to consume the accumulating `new`-status drafts and curate them into the live queue

**Open design question above both:**

- `c-moaj0a7u` — *Rule-based vs. LLM-observer vs. hybrid: what is the right evaluation shape for the overseer?*
- This matters most for *choosing which draft writs to promote* — i.e., blocks `c-moaz1q9b` going past "one-shot MVP"

**Measurement plumbing (waiting on data):**

- `c-moaz1r58` — post-hoc duplication measurement. Correctly parked until we've accumulated enough draft writs to evaluate.

## Housekeeping this conversation surfaced

1. **`c-moaz1pdw` is still `live`** but the work is shipped. Should be concluded — the sealed commit is the evidence.
2. **`c-mo39ro9x`** ("Turn analyst observations into follow-up commissions — should observations be first-class sources, and how?") is effectively answered by `c-moaz1pdw` shipping. Candidate for conclude-as-answered.
3. **Overseer → Reckoner rename.** Affects click goals (`c-moaj06ty`, `c-moaz1q9b`, `c-moaj0a7u`), the shipped brief's prose, and any `nsg overseer commission` CLI shape in the next commission. Note: click goals aren't currently editable (`c-mo1yf2y1` is the live meta-click on that). Options: (a) rename in place if/when that capability lands, (b) add a rename note/link on each affected click now, (c) just carry the new name forward in new artifacts and let the old name die organically.
4. **Notification scope boundary.** The concluded click `c-mob1w13b` casually names the Reckoner as the pulse-emitter for intervention notifications. If notifications are *not* Reckoner's responsibility, that conclusion is at least partially wrong. Worth a separate design click once we know where notifications actually belong.

## My read on "what's next"

The natural next move is drafting the **MVP Reckoner brief** — `nsg reckoner commission` (or similar) that pulls one `new`-status `brief`-typed writ and promotes it to `open`. Dumbest possible v1: FIFO, or patron picks from a list. Defer the evaluation-shape question (`c-moaj0a7u`) to a v2 that replaces the dumb picker.

Want me to start on that brief?

content: We're working on Stage 3 self-commissioning: the observation-emission half is shipped, and Reckoner (renamed from Overseer) is the consumer that promotes draft writs to open. Next action is drafting the MVP Reckoner commission brief. (disable recaps in /config)

1	---
2	description: Work with clicks — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
3	---
4	
5	# Clicks — Session-Continuity Workflow
6	
7	> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.
8	
9	A **click** is an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks are organized in a tree — children are sub-questions of their parent. Together, the click tree forms a structured record of the reasoning and decisions that guide the guild's work.
10	
11	Clicks are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity, replacing the earlier quest writ type. Key differences from quests:
12	
13	- **Goal is immutable.** Set at creation, never changes. If the framing is wrong, drop and create a new one.
14	- **Conclusion is write-once.** Set by `conclude` or `drop`, then frozen.
15	- **No body files.** Click goals are short (one sentence). Long-form exploration lives in session transcripts, joinable via session ID.
16	- **The tree is the product.** Value lives in structure (hierarchy, decomposition), not prose. Children are the todo list.
17	- **Four statuses:** `live | parked | concluded | dropped`. Simple, designed for inquiry.
18	
19	## When to open a click
20	
21	Open a click when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:
22	
23	- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
24	- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
25	- A decision is deferred pending more thought
26	- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating sub-questions, eventual conclusion
27	
28	Don't open a click for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.
29	
30	## Creating a click
31	
32	    nsg click create --goal "How should we handle X?" [--parent-id <id>]
33	
34	- The `--goal` is the question or inquiry — frame it as a question when possible.
35	- Use `--parent-id` to nest under an existing click (sub-question decomposition).
36	- The click starts in `live` status.
37	
38	### Vocabulary discovery habit
39	
40	When opening any new click, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and consider creating cross-links. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit.
41	
42	## Viewing clicks
43	
44	Two commands do the orientation work. Reach for them in this order:
45	
46	**1. Tree view** — board-level scan, the first thing you run at startup:
47	
48	    nsg click tree [--root-id <id>] [--status live] [--depth N]
49	
50	Renders the click forest with box-drawing connectors and status indicators:
51	```
52	● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
53	  ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
54	  ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
55	  └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
56	```
57	Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.
58	
59	**2. Extract a subtree** — the primary narrative-loading command, used whenever the conversation turns toward a specific area:
60	
61	    nsg click extract --id <id> [--format md|json]
62	
63	One call loads the whole subtree — goals, statuses, and conclusions for any concluded/dropped clicks — as a structured document. This is how you orient on any line of inquiry.
64	
65	**Don't do this:** don't walk a subtree by calling `show` on each child to reconstruct the narrative. That's what `extract` is for. If you find yourself running `show` three or more times in a row to understand the shape of an area, stop and run `extract` on the common ancestor instead.
66	
67	**Show a single click** — use only for single-click inspection (checking one click's links, parent, status, or conclusion):
68	
69	    nsg click show <id>
70	
71	**List with filters:**
72	
73	    nsg click list [--status live] [--status parked] [--limit 50]
74	
75	## Status transitions
76	
77	```
78	live ──> parked       (park — deliberately dormant, pick up later)
79	parked ──> live       (resume — back to active exploration)
80	live ──> concluded    (conclude — decision reached, conclusion required)
81	live ──> dropped      (drop — abandoned, reason required)
82	parked ──> concluded  (conclude directly from parked)
83	parked ──> dropped    (drop directly from parked)
84	```
85	
86	Terminal states (concluded, dropped) are immutable — no further changes allowed.
87	
88	**Park** a click (live -> parked):
89	
90	    nsg click park --id <id>
91	
92	**Resume** a parked click (parked -> live):
93	
94	    nsg click resume --id <id>
95	
96	**Conclude** a click with a decision:
97	
98	    nsg click conclude --id <id> --conclusion "We decided to..."
99	
100	**Drop** a click without a decision:
101	
102	    nsg click drop --id <id> --conclusion "Moot because..."
103	
104	### Conclusions are tight
105	
106	A conclusion is a pointer-note for future-me, not an essay for posterity. Target shape: **decision + one-line reasoning + (optional) one-clause naming the rejected alternatives.** A small paragraph, not a screenful.
107	
108	- **No markdown structure.** No headers, no tables, no "Recommendation:" sections. If a conclusion needs structure, it's too long.
109	- **Don't re-argue rejected options.** Name them in a clause ("rejected `spider.sequence` for directional ambiguity"); don't rebuild the tradeoff table.
110	- **Exploration stays in the session transcript.** The analytical work — enumerated cases, stress tests, option matrices — belongs in chat, joinable via session ID. Only the distilled outcome lands in the conclusion.
111	
112	Verbose conclusions are the main context cost of clicks. Keep them short and the tree can grow freely.
113	
114	## Sub-questions (children)
115	
116	When a click spawns distinct sub-inquiries, create child clicks:
117	
118	    nsg click create --goal "Sub-question A?" --parent-id <parent-id>
119	
120	Children are the decomposition mechanism. Resolving a parent typically means resolving or dropping its children. But there's no automatic cascading — a parent can be concluded while children are still live (e.g., "we answered the main question; remaining sub-questions are moot").
121	
122	### Enumerated decisions → enumerated children
123	
124	When the analysis inside a click naturally enumerates N items with per-item evaluations (N sources → include/exclude each, N options → pick-one across them, N candidates → accept/reject each), those evaluations are latent sub-clicks. Lift them into children rather than collapsing them into the parent's conclusion.
125	
126	**Recognition trigger:** if you catch yourself writing a conclusion that contains a per-item list with individual rationale ("source A: yes because X; source B: no because Y; …"), stop — that list is telling you the decisions want their own clicks. The parent's conclusion should name the *principle* or *summary*; each child's conclusion carries one item's rationale.
127	
128	This aligns with conclusion-discipline (keeps each conclusion tight, no inline tables) and with capture-liberally (the tree is cheap; per-item decisions are durable structural references worth having).
129	
130	## Links
131	
132	Typed links connect clicks to each other or to writs (cross-substrate):
133	
134	    nsg click link --source-id <click-id> --target-id <target-id> --link-type <type>
135	
136	Link types:
137	- `related` — lateral cross-reference
138	- `commissioned` — this click's conclusion produced a commission (target is a writ ID)
139	- `supersedes` — this click replaces the target
140	- `depends-on` — this click can't be concluded until the target is resolved
141	
142	**Commissioned links are automatic.** When a click's conclusion spawns a writ (brief posted, commission dispatched), create the `commissioned` link from the click to the writ id without asking. This preserves the click-to-artifact trail in the tree and is cheap to maintain. If multiple clicks contribute to one commission, link the narrowest conclusion-bearing click; add additional links from contributing parents only if the trail would be lossy otherwise.
143	
144	Remove a link:
145	
146	    nsg click unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>
147	
148	## Reparenting
149	
150	Move a click to a new parent or to root:
151	
152	    nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent-id <new-parent-id>
153	    nsg click reparent --id <id>                               # move to root
154	
155	Circular parentage is detected and rejected.
156	
157	## Short ID support
158	
159	All ID parameters accept short prefixes (e.g., `c-mo1ee` instead of the full ID). The Ratchet resolves prefixes and errors on ambiguity.
160	
161	## Clicks and session continuity
162	
163	Clicks provide continuity across sessions. The flow is:
164	
165	- **Startup** — scan the click tree with `nsg click tree --status live --status parked` to see what's in flight. When the conversation turns toward a specific area, use `nsg click extract --id <id>` to load the full subtree as narrative context. Reserve `nsg click show <id>` for single-click inspection. Don't eagerly read every click.
166	- **During the session** — open new clicks for substantial inquiries; create child clicks for sub-questions. Conclude or drop clicks as decisions are reached.
167	- **Wrap-up** — park any live clicks that won't be continued immediately. Conclude clicks where decisions were reached this session. The click tree itself is the session's durable record of what was explored and decided.
168	
169	Because click goals are immutable and conclusions are write-once, there's no body to maintain or keep in sync. The session transcript (joinable via session ID) holds the full exploration context. Clicks are just the structural skeleton — questions asked and answers given.
170

---
description: Work with clicks — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
---

# Clicks — Session-Continuity Workflow

> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.

A **click** is an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks are organized in a tree — children are sub-questions of their parent. Together, the click tree forms a structured record of the reasoning and decisions that guide the guild's work.

Clicks are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity, replacing the earlier quest writ type. Key differences from quests:

- **Goal is immutable.** Set at creation, never changes. If the framing is wrong, drop and create a new one.
- **Conclusion is write-once.** Set by `conclude` or `drop`, then frozen.
- **No body files.** Click goals are short (one sentence). Long-form exploration lives in session transcripts, joinable via session ID.
- **The tree is the product.** Value lives in structure (hierarchy, decomposition), not prose. Children are the todo list.
- **Four statuses:** `live | parked | concluded | dropped`. Simple, designed for inquiry.

## When to open a click

Open a click when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:

- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
- A decision is deferred pending more thought
- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating sub-questions, eventual conclusion

Don't open a click for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.

## Creating a click

    nsg click create --goal "How should we handle X?" [--parent-id <id>]

- The `--goal` is the question or inquiry — frame it as a question when possible.
- Use `--parent-id` to nest under an existing click (sub-question decomposition).
- The click starts in `live` status.

### Vocabulary discovery habit

When opening any new click, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and consider creating cross-links. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit.

## Viewing clicks

Two commands do the orientation work. Reach for them in this order:

**1. Tree view** — board-level scan, the first thing you run at startup:

    nsg click tree [--root-id <id>] [--status live] [--depth N]

Renders the click forest with box-drawing connectors and status indicators:
```
● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
  ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
  ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
  └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
```
Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.

**2. Extract a subtree** — the primary narrative-loading command, used whenever the conversation turns toward a specific area:

    nsg click extract --id <id> [--format md|json]

One call loads the whole subtree — goals, statuses, and conclusions for any concluded/dropped clicks — as a structured document. This is how you orient on any line of inquiry.

**Don't do this:** don't walk a subtree by calling `show` on each child to reconstruct the narrative. That's what `extract` is for. If you find yourself running `show` three or more times in a row to understand the shape of an area, stop and run `extract` on the common ancestor instead.

**Show a single click** — use only for single-click inspection (checking one click's links, parent, status, or conclusion):

    nsg click show <id>

**List with filters:**

    nsg click list [--status live] [--status parked] [--limit 50]

## Status transitions

```
live ──> parked       (park — deliberately dormant, pick up later)
parked ──> live       (resume — back to active exploration)
live ──> concluded    (conclude — decision reached, conclusion required)
live ──> dropped      (drop — abandoned, reason required)
parked ──> concluded  (conclude directly from parked)
parked ──> dropped    (drop directly from parked)
```

Terminal states (concluded, dropped) are immutable — no further changes allowed.

**Park** a click (live -> parked):

    nsg click park --id <id>

**Resume** a parked click (parked -> live):

    nsg click resume --id <id>

**Conclude** a click with a decision:

    nsg click conclude --id <id> --conclusion "We decided to..."

**Drop** a click without a decision:

    nsg click drop --id <id> --conclusion "Moot because..."

### Conclusions are tight

A conclusion is a pointer-note for future-me, not an essay for posterity. Target shape: **decision + one-line reasoning + (optional) one-clause naming the rejected alternatives.** A small paragraph, not a screenful.

- **No markdown structure.** No headers, no tables, no "Recommendation:" sections. If a conclusion needs structure, it's too long.
- **Don't re-argue rejected options.** Name them in a clause ("rejected `spider.sequence` for directional ambiguity"); don't rebuild the tradeoff table.
- **Exploration stays in the session transcript.** The analytical work — enumerated cases, stress tests, option matrices — belongs in chat, joinable via session ID. Only the distilled outcome lands in the conclusion.

Verbose conclusions are the main context cost of clicks. Keep them short and the tree can grow freely.

## Sub-questions (children)

When a click spawns distinct sub-inquiries, create child clicks:

    nsg click create --goal "Sub-question A?" --parent-id <parent-id>

Children are the decomposition mechanism. Resolving a parent typically means resolving or dropping its children. But there's no automatic cascading — a parent can be concluded while children are still live (e.g., "we answered the main question; remaining sub-questions are moot").

### Enumerated decisions → enumerated children

When the analysis inside a click naturally enumerates N items with per-item evaluations (N sources → include/exclude each, N options → pick-one across them, N candidates → accept/reject each), those evaluations are latent sub-clicks. Lift them into children rather than collapsing them into the parent's conclusion.

**Recognition trigger:** if you catch yourself writing a conclusion that contains a per-item list with individual rationale ("source A: yes because X; source B: no because Y; …"), stop — that list is telling you the decisions want their own clicks. The parent's conclusion should name the *principle* or *summary*; each child's conclusion carries one item's rationale.

This aligns with conclusion-discipline (keeps each conclusion tight, no inline tables) and with capture-liberally (the tree is cheap; per-item decisions are durable structural references worth having).

## Links

Typed links connect clicks to each other or to writs (cross-substrate):

    nsg click link --source-id <click-id> --target-id <target-id> --link-type <type>

Link types:
- `related` — lateral cross-reference
- `commissioned` — this click's conclusion produced a commission (target is a writ ID)
- `supersedes` — this click replaces the target
- `depends-on` — this click can't be concluded until the target is resolved

**Commissioned links are automatic.** When a click's conclusion spawns a writ (brief posted, commission dispatched), create the `commissioned` link from the click to the writ id without asking. This preserves the click-to-artifact trail in the tree and is cheap to maintain. If multiple clicks contribute to one commission, link the narrowest conclusion-bearing click; add additional links from contributing parents only if the trail would be lossy otherwise.

Remove a link:

    nsg click unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>

## Reparenting

Move a click to a new parent or to root:

    nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent-id <new-parent-id>
    nsg click reparent --id <id>                               # move to root

Circular parentage is detected and rejected.

## Short ID support

All ID parameters accept short prefixes (e.g., `c-mo1ee` instead of the full ID). The Ratchet resolves prefixes and errors on ambiguity.

## Clicks and session continuity

Clicks provide continuity across sessions. The flow is:

- **Startup** — scan the click tree with `nsg click tree --status live --status parked` to see what's in flight. When the conversation turns toward a specific area, use `nsg click extract --id <id>` to load the full subtree as narrative context. Reserve `nsg click show <id>` for single-click inspection. Don't eagerly read every click.
- **During the session** — open new clicks for substantial inquiries; create child clicks for sub-questions. Conclude or drop clicks as decisions are reached.
- **Wrap-up** — park any live clicks that won't be continued immediately. Conclude clicks where decisions were reached this session. The click tree itself is the session's durable record of what was explored and decided.

Because click goals are immutable and conclusions are write-once, there's no body to maintain or keep in sync. The session transcript (joinable via session ID) holds the full exploration context. Clicks are just the structural skeleton — questions asked and answers given.

content.text:
Both concluded. ✅ Now the actual question.

I want to push back a little on the framing, because I think "how do we amend clicks" is actually **three different problems wearing the same coat**, and a single mechanism will be awkward for all three. Let me lay them out, because the Overseer → Reckoner case is a clean example of problem #1 and would be poorly served by a generic amend/edit tool.

## Three kinds of post-hoc change

| Kind | Example | Stakes | What actually changed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Vocabulary drift** | Overseer → Reckoner | Low | The *word* we use for a stable concept |
| **2. Live-click refinement** | "Actually this question is narrower than I first wrote" | Medium | The *framing* of an in-flight inquiry |
| **3. Post-conclusion correction** | "We concluded X; turns out we were wrong / incomplete" | High | The *record* of a closed decision |

These have genuinely different audit stakes. Collapsing them into one mechanism means either over-ceremonializing #1 or under-protecting #3.

## My recommendation — differentiated mechanisms

### For (1) Vocabulary drift → a project-level alias registry, not click mutation

Keep a single YAML file — something like `docs/vocabulary/aliases.yaml`:

    - term: Reckoner
      aliases: [Overseer]
      renamed_at: 2026-04-23
      reason: "Scope clarified — self-commissioning apparatus, distinct from notifications"

**Don't mutate the clicks at all.** The old clicks said "Overseer" because that's what we called it then — that's an honest historical fact. What we add is a registry that:
- Tools can read (`nsg click search reckoner` also matches old "overseer" text).
- Readers see (when extracting a subtree that references an aliased term, print a footer: "Terms: Overseer → Reckoner (2026-04-23)").
- Future publication-time tooling can consume (when we turn click trees into blog posts, the aliases inform rewrites).

Cost: one file, occasional entries. Benefit: covers renames across *all* artifacts — clicks, briefs, commit messages, docs. Clicks aren't special in this regard, and solving it only for clicks would be a half-measure.

### For (2) Live-click refinement → enable goal editing, but only while live, with history

This is the answer to the existing meta-click `c-mo1yf2y1` ("Should click goals be editable?"):

- `nsg click amend --id <id> --goal "..."` — allowed only when `status = live`.
- Store `goal_history: [{goal, changed_at}]` on the click.
- Terminal states (concluded, dropped) refuse amend — the record is sealed.

Rationale: a live click is still being workshopped. Its *conclusion* hasn't landed, no downstream has committed to its framing. Locking the goal during this phase is strict in a way that doesn't pay for itself. But once concluded, the framing *becomes part of the record* that the conclusion references, and must not drift.

### For (3) Post-conclusion correction → new click + `supersedes` link

This is already supported — the substrate has `supersedes`. What's needed is a documented convention:

> If a concluded click turns out to have been wrong or incomplete, open a new click with `--goal` framing the updated inquiry, conclude it with the revised decision, and link `supersedes` to the old one.

Cheap, auditable, zero substrate change. The old click stays; the new click becomes the current answer; the chain is navigable via links.

## For the Overseer → Reckoner case specifically

I'd apply **(1) vocabulary drift**:

- Add a `docs/vocabulary/aliases.yaml` (or similar) with the rename entry.
- **Don't edit the existing clicks.** `c-moaj06ty` and its descendants keep saying "Overseer" — that's truthful history.
- Use "Reckoner" in all new artifacts from here forward (new clicks, the MVP Reckoner brief, commits).
- If/when we harvest click trees for publication, alias-substitute at publication time.

This sidesteps the questions of "do we also rewrite the brief text in `w-moaz6k39`?" (no — sealed record) and "what about commit `ad65df3c`?" (no — history). The registry handles them all uniformly without any mutation.

## Honest disagreement check

If your instinct is **"just make goals editable and stop overthinking it"** — the counter I'd offer is that conclusions referring to the original framing is a real property worth preserving. A concluded click's conclusion is often terse because it assumes the goal text as context. If goal drifts, conclusion readers get subtly misled. Immutability-after-conclusion earns its keep.

If your instinct is **"aliases are too ceremonious for a few renames"** — the counter is that renames aren't only a click problem. Briefs, docs, blog posts, commits all need resolution too. A registry is the one place that serves all of them.

If you want to **just do the simple thing and accept some fraying** — fine, skip the alias registry and let old terms age. I'd still argue for (2) enabling goal edits on live clicks and (3) documenting the supersedes pattern, because those come up independent of vocabulary.

## Proposed next moves

1. Open a click under the clicks-evolution umbrella (`c-mo1itggx`) capturing this three-part framing.
2. If you like (1), a tiny brief to stand up the alias registry + CLI awareness.
3. Conclude `c-mo1yf2y1` (the goal-editability meta-click) with the (2) answer: "yes for live clicks only, with history."

Where do you want to land?

I'd also like to consider the 'findability' of supersedes... they should probably always be surfaced clearly via tree/extract, regardless of parentage I would think. Conclude c-mo1yf2y1 with your understanding. Then, there is no brief for alias registry+cli awareness (sanctum artifacts). So go ahead and make those changes direclty yourself

content.content:
# Guild Vocabulary — Emerging Concepts

Latent vocabulary for the guild metaphor: concepts that are still cooking, not yet needed for bootstrap, or awaiting design decisions. Everything here is compatible with the foundational metaphor in `/workspace/nexus/docs/guild-metaphor.md` but hasn't been promoted to foundational status yet.

This document is a **tome** (in its own metaphor) — reference material to consult, not work-in-progress. When opening a new quest, scan this file for related terms and cross-link any matches in the quest's References section. That manual habit is the discovery mechanism: the vocabulary doesn't surface itself; Coco surfaces it by remembering to look.

**Active vocabulary bookmark quests** — terms with feature-shaped gaps that have earned their own parked-quest entries:

- **Coinmaster / Purse / Tithe** → cost tracking & token budget allocation (`w-mnszznt5-66d0ec7464bc`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*
- **Petition (internal-source variant)** → first-class internal commissions (`w-mnszzo8h-42137bda6681`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*. The petition concept itself is broader (any formally submitted request, patron or internal); this bookmark tracks the internal-source flow specifically.
- **Vigil** → reserved as the label for the Reckoner's oversight/maintenance/watching functions. Earlier bookmark scope (background monitoring of in-flight commissions — click `c-mo1mqgf9`, writ `w-mnszzon4-731bd9827d05`) is now one Reckoner function among several (vision-keeper `c-moa42rxh`, overseer `c-moaj06ty`, in-flight monitoring `c-mo1mqgf9`, intervention pulses `c-mo1z3teo`).

Other terms in this tome remain latent — they live here until they earn a bookmark or get absorbed into an active inquiry.

## Roles (not yet foundational)

| Role | Function | Status |
|------|----------|--------|
| **Guildmaster** | Top-level decision maker. Interfaces with the patron on behalf of the guild. Determines priorities and allocates resources. | Clear concept, not needed until guild has enough activity to require coordination. |
| **Coinmaster** | Tracks AI token balances and expenditures. Provides cost visibility and may participate in resource allocation decisions (e.g., which petitions to grant). | Real need, not blocking anything yet. |
| **Oracle** | Answers questions about code and system design. Loosely defined — a consultative role invoked when agents need understanding of existing systems. | Useful but not in the critical path. |

### Distiller

A pre-plan role that consumes a **petition** (the patron's submitted request) and produces a **brief** — a structured, six-section working artifact (Summary, Behaviors, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, Edge Cases, Open Questions) with provenance tagging on every item. The brief becomes the working contract for the **Sage** in the plan stage. The Distiller resolves product-layer ambiguity (what the feature is, who it's for, how it behaves from the user's perspective) without touching implementation-layer questions, which remain the Sage's domain. Three techniques: **extract** from the petition text, **extrapolate** from named anchors and methodological principles, **interview** (typically the Patron Anima) to fill gaps. Process discipline lives in `c-mo3qjbmw`; interview mechanics in `c-mo3qjcuw`.

Previously called BA/PO (Business Analyst / Product Owner) during initial design; renamed to Distiller because the role's essence is distilling a messy petition into a clarified, machine-parseable brief.

System mapping: a new pre-plan stage in the Astrolabe pipeline (click subtree under `c-mo3qj676`).

### Sage

The plan-stage role (formally `sage-reader-analyst`) that consumes a **brief** (Distiller's output) and produces a **spec** for the implementer. The Sage resolves implementation-layer ambiguity — APIs, schemas, workflows, structural choices — and may consult the petition as a secondary reference under disciplined conditions (see `c-mo3qsanr`). The Sage replaces what earlier design called the analyst / reader-analyst.

Pipeline placement: petition → [Distiller] → brief → [Sage] → spec → [implementer] → code.

## Work (not yet foundational)

### Petition

A formally submitted request to the guild for work to be performed. Petitions are the canonical input artifact at the front of the work pipeline — they're what the Distiller consumes to produce a brief, which the Sage then turns into a spec for implementation. The pipeline reads: petition → brief → spec → code.

Petitions may originate from either side of the guild boundary:
- **Patron-source petitions** — the patron submits a request from outside the guild (this is what the system formerly called "the brief"; renamed to clarify that the patron's submission is a request, while *brief* is reserved for the Distiller's refined output).
- **Internal-source petitions** — a guild member submits a request from within the guild for work to build or improve guild resources (paying tech debt, upgrading tooling, refactoring internal systems). Leadership evaluates internal petitions and decides which to grant, balancing cost against value. Tracked as a distinct flow under bookmark click `c-mo1mqgqv` / writ `w-mnszzo8h-42137bda6681`.

Both flavors flow through the same commission infrastructure; the originator is a `source` field on the petition, not a different artifact type. A future sub-term may distinguish the internal subset if usage warrants.

### Brief

The Distiller's output artifact — a structured, machine-parseable document derived from a petition through extract/extrapolate/interview, with provenance tagging on every item. Six sections: Summary, Behaviors, Acceptance Criteria, Out of Scope, Edge Cases, Open Questions (shape settled in `c-mo3qj9by`). The brief is the working contract for the Sage; the petition remains attached as a secondary reference (see `c-mo3qsanr`).

**Vocabulary note:** earlier design conversations and many existing clicks/commits use "brief" to mean what is now called *petition* (the patron's submitted input). The naming was inverted on `2026-04-18`; the Distiller's output is the proper "brief" in the craft sense (a polished, structured artifact prepared for an audience to act on), while the patron's submission is the petition. Historical references to "brief" in the input sense are archival; the mapping is documented here.

## Communication

### Inbox

Each guild member has an inbox — a queue where messages are delivered for the member to act on. When a message arrives, it is passed to the member's agent in context, and the member does whatever their role dictates. Inboxes are the primary mechanism for asynchronous coordination between guild entities. (Format and mechanics TBD.)

content.content:
11:- **Vigil** → reserved as the label for the Reckoner's oversight/maintenance/watching functions. Earlier bookmark scope (background monitoring of in-flight commissions — click `c-mo1mqgf9`, writ `w-mnszzon4-731bd9827d05`) is now one Reckoner function among several (vision-keeper `c-moa42rxh`, overseer `c-moaj06ty`, in-flight monitoring `c-mo1mqgf9`, intervention pulses `c-mo1z3teo`).
65:Pulses are used for intervention signals (Reckoner → patron), status reports, completion notices, and any other guild-internal or guild-to-patron transmission. Trigger-type on the pulse carries urgency — the pulse itself is a neutral container.
75:The guild's general-purpose messaging apparatus. Carries pulses between any two points — agent to agent, guild to patron, apparatus to apparatus. Delivers directly (no intermediate courier; the earlier "Herald" carrier role was dropped when this shape settled). Any part of the guild can emit a pulse through the Lattice: the Reckoner (intervention signals), Coinmaster (budget warnings), Clerk (approvals needed), Artificers (completion reports), etc.
79:### The Reckoner
157:**Vigil** — the *activity* of watching and waiting, not an actor or construct. Vigil is what is *kept* — the ongoing monitoring itself. Now reserved as a label for the Reckoner's oversight/maintenance/watching functions: the Reckoner keeps vigil over guild operational parameters, in-flight commissions, and the patron's vision. Specific implementations — what exactly is watched, how, when, by what sub-construct — are still to be designed. Not introduced to code or architecture docs yet.
159:**Sentinel** — reserved term for a single scoped watcher function within the Reckoner. A Sentinel watches one specific thing (writ state, queue depth, daemon health, purse balance, drift from vision) and emits a pulse into the Lattice when its condition fires. Plural by design: the Reckoner can host many Sentinels, each with a narrow scope, composed together into the Reckoner's overall vigil. Not introduced to code or architecture docs yet — reserved until the Reckoner's internal decomposition is firmed up.

content.content:
10-- **Petition (internal-source variant)** → first-class internal commissions (`w-mnszzo8h-42137bda6681`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*. The petition concept itself is broader (any formally submitted request, patron or internal); this bookmark tracks the internal-source flow specifically.
11:- **Vigil** → reserved as the label for the Reckoner's oversight/maintenance/watching functions. Earlier bookmark scope (background monitoring of in-flight commissions — click `c-mo1mqgf9`, writ `w-mnszzon4-731bd9827d05`) is now one Reckoner function among several (vision-keeper `c-moa42rxh`, overseer `c-moaj06ty`, in-flight monitoring `c-mo1mqgf9`, intervention pulses `c-mo1z3teo`).
12-
13-Other terms in this tome remain latent — they live here until they earn a bookmark or get absorbed into an active inquiry.
14-
15-## Roles (not yet foundational)
16-
17-| Role | Function | Status |
18-|------|----------|--------|
19-| **Guildmaster** | Top-level decision maker. Interfaces with the patron on behalf of the guild. Determines priorities and allocates resources. | Clear concept, not needed until guild has enough activity to require coordination. |
20-| **Coinmaster** | Tracks AI token balances and expenditures. Provides cost visibility and may participate in resource allocation decisions (e.g., which petitions to grant). | Real need, not blocking anything yet. |
21-| **Oracle** | Answers questions about code and system design. Loosely defined — a consultative role invoked when agents need understanding of existing systems. | Useful but not in the critical path. |
--
64-
65:Pulses are used for intervention signals (Reckoner → patron), status reports, completion notices, and any other guild-internal or guild-to-patron transmission. Trigger-type on the pulse carries urgency — the pulse itself is a neutral container.
66-
67-Punchier successor to the earlier "missive" framing; coheres better with the Lattice-as-network metaphor. The pulse is the *transmission event*, which cleanly decouples it from payload size — short signals and payload-bearing completion reports both fit.
68-
69-## Apparatuses
70-
71-Named mechanical constructs of the guild — infrastructure-level machinery with focused responsibilities, composed together to operate the guild. Distinct from *roles* (human-equivalent agents like Distiller, Sage) and from *artifacts* (records like writs, pulses, briefs).
72-
73-### The Lattice
74-
75:The guild's general-purpose messaging apparatus. Carries pulses between any two points — agent to agent, guild to patron, apparatus to apparatus. Delivers directly (no intermediate courier; the earlier "Herald" carrier role was dropped when this shape settled). Any part of the guild can emit a pulse through the Lattice: the Reckoner (intervention signals), Coinmaster (budget warnings), Clerk (approvals needed), Artificers (completion reports), etc.
76-
77-System mapping: outbox + delivery infrastructure for notifications and signals. Pulses are immutable records (durable event log, not mutable tasks) stored in the book; the Lattice reads pending-delivery pulses and ships them through configured channels. Pulses have no acknowledgment state — their referent (typically a writ) holds the live state, and "still needs attention" is derived by joining to that referent.
78-
79:### The Reckoner
80-
81-The guild's command-and-control apparatus — the executive function that senses guild state and acts on it. Responsibilities:
82-
83-- **Monitor the patron's vision** — the strategic direction the guild is meant to advance.
84-- **Monitor operational parameters** — cost, quality, queue state, daemon health, commissions in flight, drift from expected behavior.
85-- **Act on observations** via two modes:
86-  - *Ask the patron* — emit a pulse through the Lattice to surface something that needs patron judgment (commission stuck, budget exhausted, queue drained, etc.).
87-  - *Act autonomously* — post a commission to remediate a guild issue or advance the vision.
88-
89-Subsumes several existing design clicks as internal functions: c-moa42rxh (vision-keeper), c-moaj06ty (overseer pattern), c-mo1mqgf9 (background monitoring of in-flight commissions), c-mo1z3teo (intervention pulses). Specific internal decomposition (what watchers, what contracts) still to be designed.
--
156-
157:**Vigil** — the *activity* of watching and waiting, not an actor or construct. Vigil is what is *kept* — the ongoing monitoring itself. Now reserved as a label for the Reckoner's oversight/maintenance/watching functions: the Reckoner keeps vigil over guild operational parameters, in-flight commissions, and the patron's vision. Specific implementations — what exactly is watched, how, when, by what sub-construct — are still to be designed. Not introduced to code or architecture docs yet.
158-
159:**Sentinel** — reserved term for a single scoped watcher function within the Reckoner. A Sentinel watches one specific thing (writ state, queue depth, daemon health, purse balance, drift from vision) and emits a pulse into the Lattice when its condition fires. Plural by design: the Reckoner can host many Sentinels, each with a narrow scope, composed together into the Reckoner's overall vigil. Not introduced to code or architecture docs yet — reserved until the Reckoner's internal decomposition is firmed up.
160-
161-**Rite of Naming** — the ceremony by which a new anima is called into being and given their identity. Not just "add to database" — the naming is when the spirit receives its name and seal, becoming a distinct presence in the guild. The rite could include an initial training session at the Academy (aspirant phase), a trial by craft, and formal induction to the roster as an active anima.
162-
163-**Requiem** — the process of retiring an anima. Not dissolution — the anima's record persists in the register forever, their seals remain on their work, their lore is preserved. But the spirit passes to rest: `retired` state, no longer manifested. System mapping: a formal process that includes archiving the anima's current instructions and recording why they were retired.
164-
165-### Communication & Coordination
166-
167-**Herald** — *(dropped from active vocabulary.)* Earlier proposed as a carrier/delivery construct for guild announcements. When the messaging apparatus settled as **the Lattice** (which delivers pulses directly, without an intermediate courier role), Herald fell out — the separation between "the apparatus" and "the carrier" collapsed into just the apparatus. Preserved here in case a channel-specific or role-bound courier concept re-emerges later.
168-
169-**Summons** — a formal request for a specific member's attention. Different from a message in the inbox — a summons is urgent and targeted. "The Oracle is summoned to examine the authentication module." System mapping: a high-priority, synchronous invocation of a specific member, as opposed to async inbox delivery. *(Naming conflict resolved — "summon" removed from foundational metaphor, engine renamed to "compose", later to "manifest.")*

input.command: nsg click conclude --id c-mo1yf2y1 --conclusion "Yes, but only while live. Goals should be editable via 'nsg click amend --goal' while status=live, with goal_history preserved on the click for audit. Terminal states (concluded, dropped) seal the goal — at conclusion the decision commits to the current framing, and later drift would retcon the record. Vocabulary drift is a separate concern handled by alias registry (c-mobzw7uc), not click mutation. Post-conclusion corrections use a new click + 'supersedes' link (c-mobzw9of). Implementation tracked under c-mobzw8pn."
